  6→/**
  7→ * Creates a context for a new coroutine. It installs [Dispatchers.Default] when no other dispatcher or
  8→ * [ContinuationInterceptor] is specified and adds optional support for debugging facilities (when turned on)
  9→ * and copyable-thread-local facilities on JVM.
 10→ * See [DEBUG_PROPERTY_NAME] for description of debugging facilities on JVM.
 11→ */
 12→@ExperimentalCoroutinesApi
 13→public actual fun CoroutineScope.newCoroutineContext(context: CoroutineContext): CoroutineContext {
 14→    val combined = foldCopies(coroutineContext, context, true)
 15→    val debug = if (DEBUG) combined + CoroutineId(COROUTINE_ID.incrementAndGet()) else combined
 16→    return if (combined !== Dispatchers.Default && combined[ContinuationInterceptor] == null)
 17→        debug + Dispatchers.Default else debug

 20→/**
 21→ * Creates a context for coroutine builder functions that do not launch a new coroutine, e.g. [withContext].
 22→ * @suppress
 23→ */
 24→@InternalCoroutinesApi
 25→public actual fun CoroutineContext.newCoroutineContext(addedContext: CoroutineContext): CoroutineContext {
 26→    /*
 27→     * Fast-path: we only have to copy/merge if 'addedContext' (which typically has one or two elements)
 28→     * contains copyable elements.
 29→     */
 30→    if (!addedContext.hasCopyableElements()) return this + addedContext
 31→    return foldCopies(this, addedContext, false)
 32→}
 33→
 34→private fun CoroutineContext.hasCopyableElements(): Boolean =
 35→    fold(false) { result, it -> result || it is CopyableThreadContextElement<*> }
 36→
 37→/**
 38→ * Folds two contexts properly applying [CopyableThreadContextElement] rules when necessary.
 39→ * The rules are the following:
 40→ * - If neither context has CTCE, the sum of two contexts is returned
 41→ * - Every CTCE from the left-hand side context that does not have a matching (by key) element from right-hand side context
 42→ *   is [copied][CopyableThreadContextElement.copyForChild] if [isNewCoroutine] is `true`.
 43→ * - Every CTCE from the left-hand side context that has a matching element in the right-hand side context is [merged][CopyableThreadContextElement.mergeForChild]
 44→ * - Every CTCE from the right-hand side context that hasn't been merged is copied
 45→ * - Everything else is added to the resulting context as is.
 46→ */
 47→private fun foldCopies(originalContext: CoroutineContext, appendContext: CoroutineContext, isNewCoroutine: Boolean): CoroutineContext {
 48→    // Do we have something to copy left-hand side?
 49→    val hasElementsLeft = originalContext.hasCopyableElements()
 50→    val hasElementsRight = appendContext.hasCopyableElements()
 51→
 52→    // Nothing to fold, so just return the sum of contexts
 53→    if (!hasElementsLeft && !hasElementsRight) {
 54→        return originalContext + appendContext
 55→    }
 56→
 57→    var leftoverContext = appendContext
 58→    val folded = originalContext.fold<CoroutineContext>(EmptyCoroutineContext) { result, element ->
 59→        if (element !is CopyableThreadContextElement<*>) return@fold result + element
 60→        // Will this element be overwritten?
 61→        val newElement = leftoverContext[element.key]
 62→        // No, just copy it
 63→        if (newElement == null) {
 64→            // For 'withContext'-like builders we do not copy as the element is not shared
 65→            return@fold result + if (isNewCoroutine) element.copyForChild() else element
 66→        }
 67→        // Yes, then first remove the element from append context
 68→        leftoverContext = leftoverContext.minusKey(element.key)
 69→        // Return the sum
 70→        @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST")
 71→        return@fold result + (element as CopyableThreadContextElement<Any?>).mergeForChild(newElement)
 72→    }
 73→
 74→    if (hasElementsRight) {
 75→        leftoverContext = leftoverContext.fold<CoroutineContext>(EmptyCoroutineContext) { result, element ->
 76→            // We're appending new context element -- we have to copy it, otherwise it may be shared with others
 77→            if (element is CopyableThreadContextElement<*>) {
 78→                return@fold result + element.copyForChild()
 79→            }
 80→            return@fold result + element
 81→        }
 82→    }
 83→    return folded + leftoverContext
 84→}

 89→internal actual inline fun <T> withCoroutineContext(context: CoroutineContext, countOrElement: Any?, block: () -> T): T {
 90→    val oldValue = updateThreadContext(context, countOrElement)
 91→    try {
 92→        return block()
 93→    } finally {
 94→        restoreThreadContext(context, oldValue)
 95→    }
 96→}

 98→/**
 99→ * Executes a block using a context of a given continuation.
100→ */
101→internal actual inline fun <T> withContinuationContext(continuation: Continuation<*>, countOrElement: Any?, block: () -> T): T {
102→    val context = continuation.context
103→    val oldValue = updateThreadContext(context, countOrElement)
104→    val undispatchedCompletion = if (oldValue !== NO_THREAD_ELEMENTS) {
105→        // Only if some values were replaced we'll go to the slow path of figuring out where/how to restore them
106→        continuation.updateUndispatchedCompletion(context, oldValue)
107→    } else {
108→        null // fast path -- don't even try to find undispatchedCompletion as there's nothing to restore in the context
109→    }
110→    try {
111→        return block()
112→    } finally {
113→        if (undispatchedCompletion == null || undispatchedCompletion.clearThreadContext()) {
114→            restoreThreadContext(context, oldValue)
115→        }
116→    }
117→}

119→internal fun Continuation<*>.updateUndispatchedCompletion(context: CoroutineContext, oldValue: Any?): UndispatchedCoroutine<*>? {
120→    if (this !is CoroutineStackFrame) return null
121→    /*
122→     * Fast-path to detect whether we have undispatched coroutine at all in our stack.
123→     *
124→     * Implementation note.
125→     * If we ever find that stackwalking for thread-locals is way too slow, here is another idea:
126→     * 1) Store undispatched coroutine right in the `UndispatchedMarker` instance
127→     * 2) To avoid issues with cross-dispatch boundary, remove `UndispatchedMarker`
128→     *    from the context when creating dispatched coroutine in `withContext`.
129→     *    Another option is to "unmark it" instead of removing to save an allocation.
130→     *    Both options should work, but it requires more careful studying of the performance
131→     *    and, mostly, maintainability impact.
132→     */
133→    val potentiallyHasUndispatchedCoroutine = context[UndispatchedMarker] !== null
134→    if (!potentiallyHasUndispatchedCoroutine) return null
135→    val completion = undispatchedCompletion()
136→    completion?.saveThreadContext(context, oldValue)
137→    return completion
138→}

140→internal tailrec fun CoroutineStackFrame.undispatchedCompletion(): UndispatchedCoroutine<*>? {
141→    // Find direct completion of this continuation
142→    val completion: CoroutineStackFrame = when (this) {
143→        is DispatchedCoroutine<*> -> return null
144→        else -> callerFrame ?: return null // something else -- not supported
145→    }
146→    if (completion is UndispatchedCoroutine<*>) return completion // found UndispatchedCoroutine!
147→    return completion.undispatchedCompletion() // walk up the call stack with tail call
148→}

155→    override val key: CoroutineContext.Key<*>

159→// Used by withContext when context changes, but dispatcher stays the same
160→internal actual class UndispatchedCoroutine<in T>actual constructor (
161→    context: CoroutineContext,
162→    uCont: Continuation<T>
163→) : ScopeCoroutine<T>(if (context[UndispatchedMarker] == null) context + UndispatchedMarker else context, uCont) {
164→
165→    /**
166→     * The state of [ThreadContextElement]s associated with the current undispatched coroutine.
167→     * It is stored in a thread local because this coroutine can be used concurrently in suspend-resume race scenario.
168→     * See the followin, boiled down example with inlined `withContinuationContext` body:
169→     * ```
170→     * val state = saveThreadContext(ctx)
171→     * try {
172→     *     invokeSmthWithThisCoroutineAsCompletion() // Completion implies that 'afterResume' will be called
173→     *     // COROUTINE_SUSPENDED is returned
174→     * } finally {
175→     *     thisCoroutine().clearThreadContext() // Concurrently the "smth" could've been already resumed on a different thread
176→     *     // and it also calls saveThreadContext and clearThreadContext
177→     * }
178→     * ```
179→     *
180→     * Usage note:
181→     *
182→     * This part of the code is performance-sensitive.
183→     * It is a well-established pattern to wrap various activities into system-specific undispatched
184→     * `withContext` for the sake of logging, MDC, tracing etc., meaning that there exists thousands of
185→     * undispatched coroutines.
186→     * Each access to Java's [ThreadLocal] leaves a footprint in the corresponding Thread's `ThreadLocalMap`
187→     * that is cleared automatically as soon as the associated thread-local (-> UndispatchedCoroutine) is garbage collected.
188→     * When such coroutines are promoted to old generation, `ThreadLocalMap`s become bloated and an arbitrary accesses to thread locals
189→     * start to consume significant amount of CPU because these maps are open-addressed and cleaned up incrementally on each access.
190→     * (You can read more about this effect as "GC nepotism").
191→     *
192→     * To avoid that, we attempt to narrow down the lifetime of this thread local as much as possible:
193→     * - It's never accessed when we are sure there are no thread context elements
194→     * - It's cleaned up via [ThreadLocal.remove] as soon as the coroutine is suspended or finished.
195→     */
196→    private val threadStateToRecover = ThreadLocal<Pair<CoroutineContext, Any?>>()
197→
198→    /*
199→     * Indicates that a coroutine has at least one thread context element associated with it
200→     * and that 'threadStateToRecover' is going to be set in case of dispatchhing in order to preserve them.
201→     * Better than nullable thread-local for easier debugging.
202→     *
203→     * It is used as a performance optimization to avoid 'threadStateToRecover' initialization
204→     * (note: tl.get() initializes thread local),
205→     * and is prone to false-positives as it is never reset: otherwise
206→     * it may lead to logical data races between suspensions point where
207→     * coroutine is yet being suspended in one thread while already being resumed
208→     * in another.
209→     */
210→    @Volatile
211→    private var threadLocalIsSet = false
212→
213→    init {
214→        /*
215→         * This is a hack for a very specific case in #2930 unless #3253 is implemented.
216→         * 'ThreadLocalStressTest' covers this change properly.
217→         *
218→         * The scenario this change covers is the following:
219→         * 1) The coroutine is being started as plain non kotlinx.coroutines related suspend function,
220→         *    e.g. `suspend fun main` or, more importantly, Ktor `SuspendFunGun`, that is invoking
221→         *    `withContext(tlElement)` which creates `UndispatchedCoroutine`.
222→         * 2) It (original continuation) is then not wrapped into `DispatchedContinuation` via `intercept()`
223→         *    and goes neither through `DC.run` nor through `resumeUndispatchedWith` that both
224→         *    do thread context element tracking.
225→         * 3) So thread locals never got chance to get properly set up via `saveThreadContext`,
226→         *    but when `withContext` finishes, it attempts to recover thread locals in its `afterResume`.
227→         *
228→         * Here we detect precisely this situation and properly setup context to recover later.
229→         *
230→         */
231→        if (uCont.context[ContinuationInterceptor] !is CoroutineDispatcher) {
232→            /*
233→             * We cannot just "read" the elements as there is no such API,
234→             * so we update-restore it immediately and use the intermediate value
235→             * as the initial state, leveraging the fact that thread context element
236→             * is idempotent and such situations are increasingly rare.
237→             */
238→            val values = updateThreadContext(context, null)
239→            restoreThreadContext(context, values)
240→            saveThreadContext(context, values)
241→        }
242→    }
243→
244→    fun saveThreadContext(context: CoroutineContext, oldValue: Any?) {
245→        threadLocalIsSet = true // Specify that thread-local is touched at all
246→        threadStateToRecover.set(context to oldValue)
247→    }
248→
249→    fun clearThreadContext(): Boolean {
250→        return !(threadLocalIsSet && threadStateToRecover.get() == null).also {
251→            threadStateToRecover.remove()
252→        }
253→    }
254→
255→    override fun afterResume(state: Any?) {
256→        if (threadLocalIsSet) {
257→            threadStateToRecover.get()?.let { (ctx, value) ->
258→                restoreThreadContext(ctx, value)
259→            }
260→            threadStateToRecover.remove()
261→        }
262→        // resume undispatched -- update context but stay on the same dispatcher
263→        val result = recoverResult(state, uCont)
264→        withContinuationContext(uCont, null) {
265→            uCont.resumeWith(result)
266→        }
267→    }
268→}
